meta.name: OAuth Attacks
meta.description: OAuth 2.0 and OIDC attack patterns covering redirect_uri bypass, state parameter abuse, token theft, PKCE bypass, authorization code reuse, and open redirect chaining.

payloads.description: Open redirect_uri — server accepts any redirect_uri, attacker receives the access token directly
Subdomain confusion — add attacker domain as subdomain of whitelisted domain
Path traversal in redirect_uri — escape whitelisted path using encoded traversal sequences
At-sign confusion in redirect_uri — browser interprets attacker.com as the host
Open redirect chaining — use an open redirect on the legitimate domain to forward to attacker
Missing state parameter — CSRF on OAuth flow; no state = no CSRF protection, attacker initiates auth as victim
Predictable state parameter — if state is not random/unique, replay to bind victim's code to attacker session
Authorization code replay — reuse code intercepted via referer leakage or redirect_uri manipulation
Implicit flow with ID token — request both access and ID tokens in URL fragment, useful for token extraction
Scope escalation — request additional scopes (offline_access for refresh tokens) not normally granted
redirect_uri mismatch in token exchange — some servers don't validate redirect_uri at token exchange stage
Refresh token theft — long-lived refresh tokens if stolen allow persistent access after access token expires
PKCE downgrade — force plain method instead of S256 if server allows; exposes code_challenge for replay
None algorithm JWT as Bearer — if resource server doesn't validate alg, accepts unsigned token as valid
Client credential brute-force — weak client_secret allows attacker to generate valid tokens as that client
Malicious OAuth app — register app requesting elevated scopes; if auto-approved, victim silently grants access
Silent re-authentication bypass — use prompt=none with a valid id_token_hint to get code without user interaction
JWT bearer grant type — forge assertion JWT if server uses weak signing; exchange for access token without user
Predictable nonce in OIDC — static/guessable nonce allows replay of ID tokens across sessions
Authorization code leakage via Referer — if redirect target loads external resources, code leaks in Referer header
